stem: APAP toxicity causes liver damage via:

choices.feedback: APAP itself is not directly toxic.
APAP does not cause biliary obstruction.

choices.text: NAPQI accumulation when glutathione is depleted
choices.feedback: Correct! CYP2E1 converts APAP → NAPQI (toxic). Normally, glutathione neutralizes NAPQI. In overdose: glutathione depleted → NAPQI accumulates → centrilobular hepatic necrosis (zone 3). NAC works by replenishing glutathione stores and providing alternative substrate for NAPQI conjugation.
APAP toxicity is direct, not autoimmune.

explanation: Acetaminophen hepatotoxicity is caused by NAPQI (N-acetyl-p-benzoquinone imine), a toxic metabolite produced by CYP2E1. At therapeutic doses, NAPQI is detoxified by conjugation with glutathione. In overdose, glutathione stores are depleted (below ~30% of normal), allowing NAPQI to bind hepatocyte proteins and cause centrilobular necrosis. NAC (N-acetylcysteine) works by replenishing glutathione, providing alternative sulfhydryl groups for NAPQI detoxification, and enhancing sulfate conjugation of acetaminophen. The toxic dose threshold is generally 150 mg/kg in a single ingestion.

stem: APAP overdose: patient feels fine at 12 hours. Is this reassuring?

choices.text: Yes, if the ingestion was less than 10g
choices.feedback: Even at 7.5g, hepatotoxicity can develop.

choices.text: Yes — symptoms would have appeared by now
choices.feedback: Dangerously false! Stage I (0-24h) is deceptively asymptomatic.

choices.text: No — Stage I is asymptomatic despite ongoing hepatic damage
choices.feedback: Correct! The 'deceptive well period' is a hallmark of APAP toxicity. Patients feel well during Stage I (0-24h) while NAPQI is silently destroying hepatocytes. By Stage II (24-72h), AST/ALT spike and RUQ pain develops. This is why the nomogram and early NAC are critical — don't wait for symptoms.

choices.text: Only reassuring if liver enzymes are checked
choices.feedback: Liver enzymes may be normal in Stage I despite ongoing damage. APAP level and nomogram guide treatment.

explanation: Stage I of acetaminophen toxicity (0-24 hours) is deceptively asymptomatic or causes only mild nonspecific symptoms (nausea, vomiting, malaise). Patients may feel completely well. However, hepatocellular damage is already occurring as NAPQI accumulates and depletes glutathione. Stage II (24-72 hours) shows rising AST/ALT and RUQ pain. Stage III (72-96 hours) is peak hepatotoxicity with potential fulminant liver failure. Stage IV (4-14 days) is recovery or death. The asymptomatic Stage I is clinically treacherous because patients and clinicians may underestimate the ingestion's severity.

stem: NAC is most effective when given within:

choices.feedback: While NAC has benefit even late, efficacy drops significantly after 8h.

Correct! NAC within 8 hours → nearly 100% prevention of hepatotoxicity. Still beneficial after 8h (reduces severity). Even in established liver failure (Stage III), NAC improves outcomes and reduces mortality. Never 'too late' to give NAC if there's evidence of toxicity. Key: don't delay NAC waiting for nomogram results if >8h have passed.
While earlier is always better, the critical window is wider.
NAC works by multiple mechanisms including replenishing glutathione even after NAPQI has formed.

explanation: NAC is most effective when given within 8 hours of acute acetaminophen ingestion — when administered in this window, it virtually eliminates the risk of hepatotoxicity. Efficacy decreases progressively after 8 hours but NAC still provides benefit at any point, even after liver failure has developed (by improving hemodynamics, oxygen delivery, and mitochondrial function). The Rumack-Matthew nomogram plots serum APAP level against time post-ingestion to determine treatment threshold; a level above the treatment line at any time point warrants NAC. Both IV (21-hour protocol) and oral (72-hour protocol) NAC regimens are effective.

stem: The Rumack-Matthew nomogram is NOT valid for:

choices.feedback: Age doesn't affect nomogram validity.

choices.text: Single acute ingestions with known time
choices.feedback: This is exactly what the nomogram IS designed for.
The nomogram applies regardless of reported dose.

choices.text: Chronic/repeated ingestions or unknown time of ingestion
choices.feedback: Correct! The nomogram is only valid for single acute ingestions with known time (plot level at 4-24h post-ingestion). Invalid for: chronic/repeated dosing, unknown time, extended-release APAP, co-ingestion delaying absorption. In these cases → treat empirically with NAC if any concern for significant ingestion.

explanation: The Rumack-Matthew nomogram is valid only for single acute acetaminophen ingestions with a known time of ingestion. It is NOT valid for: chronic/repeated ingestions (staggered dosing over time), unknown time of ingestion, sustained-release formulations, or presentations >24 hours post-ingestion. In these situations, NAC should be started empirically based on clinical judgment, APAP level, and liver function tests. The nomogram starts at 4 hours post-ingestion (earlier levels are unreliable due to ongoing absorption) and plots a treatment line from 150 mcg/mL at 4 hours to 4.7 mcg/mL at 24 hours.

stem: Which zone of the liver is most affected in APAP toxicity?

choices.feedback: APAP toxicity has a specific centrilobular (zone 3) pattern.
Zone 2 is the least commonly affected in toxicology.
Zone 1 is affected in phosphorus poisoning and eclampsia.

Correct! Zone 3 (centrilobular, around central vein) has the highest concentration of CYP2E1 → most NAPQI production → greatest oxidative damage. Pathology: centrilobular hepatic necrosis. AST/ALT can exceed 10,000 IU/L (very high transaminases are characteristic of APAP toxicity and ischemic hepatitis).

explanation: Zone 3 (centrilobular region) of the hepatic acinus is most affected in acetaminophen toxicity because CYP2E1, the enzyme that converts APAP to the toxic metabolite NAPQI, is concentrated in zone 3 hepatocytes. Zone 3 is also the most vulnerable to ischemic injury due to its position at the end of the sinusoidal blood flow (lowest oxygen tension). This creates a double hit: highest NAPQI production and lowest oxygen supply. Histologically, centrilobular necrosis is the hallmark of APAP toxicity. In contrast, zone 1 (periportal) injury is seen in phosphorus poisoning and eclampsia.

stem: King's College Criteria for liver transplant in APAP-induced ALF include:

choices.feedback: Age is a factor in non-APAP King's criteria but not the primary APAP criteria.
High AST alone doesn't meet criteria.
APAP level doesn't directly predict need for transplant.

Correct! King's College Criteria for APAP-ALF: arterial pH <7.30 (refractory acidosis despite resuscitation) is independently sufficient. OR all three of: PT >100s (INR >6.5), Cr >3.4, grade III-IV encephalopathy. Meeting criteria → urgent transplant evaluation.

explanation: King's College Criteria predict the need for liver transplantation in acetaminophen-induced acute liver failure. The criteria are: arterial pH <7.30 after adequate fluid resuscitation (single strongest predictor), OR all three of: INR >6.5 (PT >100 seconds), creatinine >3.4 mg/dL, and grade III-IV hepatic encephalopathy. Meeting these criteria carries a mortality exceeding 90% without transplantation. Other poor prognostic markers include lactate >3.5 mmol/L after early fluid resuscitation, rising lactate despite treatment, and factor V level <10%. Early referral to a transplant center is essential when trajectory suggests progression to ALF.

stem: Chronic alcoholics have increased APAP toxicity risk because:

choices.feedback: The key mechanism is CYP2E1 induction and glutathione depletion.
Alcohol doesn't significantly increase APAP absorption.
NAC metabolism is not significantly affected by alcohol use.

Correct! Chronic alcohol: CYP2E1 upregulation (more NAPQI produced) + chronic malnutrition depletes glutathione → double hit. Toxicity can occur at lower APAP doses (<4g/day). Paradoxically, acute alcohol co-ingestion may be protective (competes for CYP2E1). Chronic use is the risk factor.

explanation: Chronic alcoholics have increased susceptibility to acetaminophen hepatotoxicity through CYP2E1 enzyme induction. Chronic ethanol consumption upregulates CYP2E1, increasing the proportion of APAP metabolized to toxic NAPQI. Additionally, alcoholics often have depleted glutathione stores (from malnutrition and chronic oxidative stress), reducing their capacity to detoxify NAPQI. This means hepatotoxicity can occur at therapeutic or near-therapeutic doses in chronic alcoholics — the so-called 'therapeutic misadventure.' Some guidelines suggest lower treatment thresholds for chronic alcohol users, though the exact risk increase is debated.

stem: Anaphylactoid reaction during IV NAC should be managed by:

choices.text: Giving epinephrine and stopping all treatment
choices.feedback: Epinephrine may be needed for severe reactions, but NAC should be continued.
Charcoal is for GI decontamination, not a substitute for NAC.

choices.text: Slowing the infusion rate and giving antihistamines
choices.feedback: Correct! IV NAC anaphylactoid reactions (flushing, urticaria, bronchospasm) are rate-related (most common during loading dose). Management: pause or slow infusion, give diphenhydramine ± albuterol for bronchospasm, then restart at slower rate. True IgE-mediated anaphylaxis to NAC is extremely rare. Do not withhold NAC for anaphylactoid symptoms.
Do not permanently discontinue — the benefit of NAC outweighs anaphylactoid risk.

explanation: Anaphylactoid reactions during IV NAC administration (flushing, urticaria, bronchospasm, hypotension) occur in approximately 10-20% of patients, most commonly during the initial loading dose. Management is: slow or temporarily stop the infusion, administer antihistamines (diphenhydramine, famotidine), and for bronchospasm add inhaled bronchodilators. Epinephrine is reserved for severe anaphylaxis with hemodynamic instability. The reaction is rate-related (not true IgE-mediated allergy), so the infusion can typically be restarted at a slower rate after symptoms resolve. IV NAC should NOT be permanently discontinued due to an anaphylactoid reaction — the benefit of hepatoprotection outweighs the manageable reaction risk.

stem: Delirium tremens typically occurs at what time after last drink?

choices.feedback: Withdrawal takes hours to develop after cessation.

Correct! DT peaks at 48-96h (can be later). Timeline: minor withdrawal (6-12h) → hallucinosis (12-24h) → seizures (24-48h) → DT (48-96h). DT features: delirium + autonomic storm + hallucinations. 5-15% mortality untreated. Requires ICU-level care.
By 7-10 days, most withdrawal has resolved.
6-12h = minor withdrawal symptoms (tremor, anxiety, diaphoresis).

explanation: Delirium tremens (DT) typically occurs 48-96 hours after the last drink, though it can present up to 7 days post-cessation. DT represents the most severe form of alcohol withdrawal, characterized by profound autonomic instability (tachycardia, hypertension, hyperthermia, diaphoresis), altered sensorium with disorientation, and hallucinations (often visual — classically tactile hallucinations of insects). DT carries mortality of 5-15% even with treatment (historically up to 35% untreated). Risk factors include prior DT episodes, concurrent illness, older age, prolonged/heavy alcohol use, and elevated CIWA scores. The alcohol withdrawal timeline progresses: tremor (6-12h), seizures (12-48h), hallucinations (12-48h), DT (48-96h).

stem: First-line treatment for alcohol withdrawal?

choices.feedback: Correct! BZDs are first-line: cross-tolerance with alcohol at GABA-A receptor. Long-acting (diazepam, chlordiazepoxide) provide smoother withdrawal. Lorazepam preferred in liver failure (no hepatic metabolism). Symptom-triggered (CIWA-based) dosing is preferred over fixed-schedule — lower total dose, shorter treatment.
Antipsychotics lower seizure threshold. Only adjunct for agitation after BZDs.
SSRIs treat depression/anxiety but have no role in acute withdrawal management.
Phenytoin is NOT effective for alcohol withdrawal seizures.

explanation: Benzodiazepines are the first-line treatment for alcohol withdrawal across all severity levels. They work by enhancing GABA-A receptor activity, counteracting the CNS hyperexcitability caused by withdrawal-related GABA downregulation and glutamate/NMDA upregulation. Symptom-triggered dosing using the CIWA-Ar protocol is preferred over fixed-schedule dosing — it results in less total benzodiazepine administered and shorter treatment duration. Long-acting agents (diazepam, chlordiazepoxide) provide smoother control with self-tapering pharmacokinetics. Short-acting agents (lorazepam) are preferred in liver failure. Benzodiazepines prevent progression to seizures and DT when dosed appropriately.

stem: Why give thiamine BEFORE glucose?

choices.text: Glucose metabolism consumes thiamine → can precipitate Wernicke encephalopathy
choices.feedback: Correct! Glucose metabolism requires thiamine as a cofactor (pyruvate dehydrogenase, transketolase). IV glucose in a thiamine-depleted patient → accelerates remaining thiamine depletion → acute Wernicke encephalopathy (confusion + ataxia + ophthalmoplegia). Give thiamine IV first. This order is critical.
Glucose doesn't destroy thiamine.

choices.text: Glucose causes hyperkalemia unless thiamine is given
choices.feedback: This interaction doesn't exist.
Thiamine doesn't affect glucose absorption.

explanation: Thiamine must be given BEFORE or concurrent with glucose in alcoholic patients because glucose metabolism through pyruvate dehydrogenase and the Krebs cycle requires thiamine as a cofactor. In thiamine-depleted patients (chronic alcoholics have depleted stores), a glucose load increases metabolic demand for thiamine, potentially exhausting the last remaining reserves and precipitating acute Wernicke encephalopathy (confusion, oculomotor dysfunction, ataxia). Wernicke encephalopathy is a medical emergency — untreated, it progresses to Korsakoff syndrome (irreversible amnestic disorder). IV thiamine should be given empirically to all patients with suspected alcohol use disorder.

stem: Alcoholic hallucinosis differs from DT by:

choices.feedback: Both have hallucinations — that's the name.
DT has SEVERE autonomic instability (tachycardia, hypertension, hyperthermia).
Both can have visual hallucinations.

choices.text: Hallucinosis: CLEAR sensorium. DT: IMPAIRED sensorium (delirium)
choices.feedback: Correct! Alcoholic hallucinosis (12-24h): vivid hallucinations but patient is oriented, conversational, and knows the hallucinations aren't real. DT (48-96h): TRUE delirium — disorientation, confusion, agitation, fluctuating consciousness, plus hallucinations. DT is far more dangerous.

explanation: Alcoholic hallucinosis is distinguished from delirium tremens by a CLEAR sensorium. In alcoholic hallucinosis (occurring 12-48 hours after last drink), the patient experiences vivid hallucinations (typically auditory, sometimes visual) but remains oriented, alert, and cognitively intact — they know the hallucinations are not real. In contrast, delirium tremens (48-96 hours) features impaired sensorium with disorientation, confusion, agitation, and the patient cannot distinguish hallucinations from reality. Alcoholic hallucinosis generally resolves within 24-48 hours and carries a much better prognosis than DT. Treatment is benzodiazepines and supportive care.

stem: Which benzodiazepine is preferred in liver failure?

choices.feedback: Diazepam has active metabolites requiring hepatic metabolism (long-acting → accumulation in liver failure).

Correct! LOT mnemonic: Lorazepam, Oxazepam, Temazepam — no hepatic CYP metabolism (glucuronidation only), no active metabolites. Preferred in liver dysfunction. Diazepam and chlordiazepoxide have active hepatically-metabolized metabolites → risk of accumulation and prolonged sedation.
Midazolam is hepatically metabolized via CYP3A4.
Chlordiazepoxide has multiple active metabolites requiring hepatic metabolism.

explanation: Lorazepam is the preferred benzodiazepine in patients with significant liver disease (cirrhosis, hepatitis) because it undergoes phase II hepatic metabolism only (direct glucuronidation) — it has no active metabolites and its clearance is minimally affected by liver dysfunction. In contrast, diazepam and chlordiazepoxide undergo phase I oxidative metabolism (CYP450-dependent) to produce long-acting active metabolites (desmethyldiazepam, demoxepam) that accumulate in liver failure, causing prolonged sedation and respiratory depression. Oxazepam and temazepam also undergo only glucuronidation and are alternatives, but lorazepam is available IV, making it the most practical choice.

stem: CIWA-Ar score >20 indicates:

choices.text: Severe withdrawal with high DT risk
choices.feedback: Correct! CIWA-Ar >20: severe withdrawal requiring aggressive benzodiazepine treatment. High risk for progression to seizures and DT. ICU monitoring recommended. CIWA-based symptom-triggered dosing: administer BZDs when score ≥10, reassess q1h. This approach results in less total medication use and shorter treatment.
BZDs are always first-line. Antipsychotics lower seizure threshold.
<10 is mild. >20 is severe.

choices.text: Patient is safe for outpatient detox
choices.feedback: >20 is too severe for outpatient management.

explanation: A CIWA-Ar (Clinical Institute Withdrawal Assessment for Alcohol — revised) score >20 indicates severe alcohol withdrawal with high risk of progression to delirium tremens and seizures. The CIWA-Ar rates 10 domains (nausea/vomiting, tremor, paroxysmal sweats, anxiety, agitation, tactile/auditory/visual disturbances, headache, orientation/clouding) on a scale of 0-67. Scores <10: mild withdrawal, may not require pharmacotherapy. 10-18: moderate withdrawal, treat with benzodiazepines. >20: severe withdrawal, aggressive benzodiazepine dosing required, consider ICU monitoring. Symptom-triggered protocols use CIWA scores assessed every 1-2 hours to guide benzodiazepine administration.

stem: Phenobarbital is useful in alcohol withdrawal because:

choices.feedback: It treats acute withdrawal but doesn't prevent seizures permanently.
Phenobarbital is a potent sedative.

choices.text: It directly activates GABA receptors even when BZD-resistant
choices.feedback: Correct! Phenobarbital is a barbiturate that directly opens GABA-A chloride channels (allosteric agonist) — works even when BZD binding sites are downregulated in severe withdrawal. Increasingly used as first-line or adjunct in severe/refractory withdrawal. Longer half-life provides steady-state coverage. Risk: respiratory depression.

choices.text: It treats the underlying liver disease
choices.feedback: Phenobarbital has no effect on liver disease treatment.

explanation: Phenobarbital is useful in alcohol withdrawal because it directly activates GABA-A receptors at a different binding site than benzodiazepines, providing efficacy even when benzodiazepine tolerance or resistance develops. Benzodiazepines are allosteric modulators that enhance GABA's effect (they require GABA to be present). Barbiturates can open chloride channels independently of GABA at higher doses — this makes phenobarbital effective for benzodiazepine-resistant alcohol withdrawal. It has a long half-life (80-120 hours) providing sustained symptom control. Phenobarbital is increasingly used as primary therapy or adjunctive therapy for severe withdrawal, particularly in ICU settings.

stem: Kindling effect in alcohol withdrawal refers to:

choices.feedback: Kindling is not related to temperature.

choices.text: The calming effect of the first drink
choices.feedback: That's tolerance/dependence, not kindling.

choices.text: A specific type of seizure unique to alcoholism
choices.feedback: Kindling is a concept, not a seizure type.

choices.text: Each successive withdrawal episode becomes more severe due to neuronal sensitization
choices.feedback: Correct! Repeated withdrawal episodes → progressive neuronal sensitization (NMDA receptor upregulation) → each subsequent withdrawal is more severe with higher seizure risk and DT risk. This is why patients with multiple prior detoxifications are at highest risk. Supports the argument for adequate treatment of each withdrawal episode.

explanation: The kindling effect in alcohol withdrawal refers to the phenomenon where each successive withdrawal episode becomes more severe than the previous one. This occurs through neuroadaptive changes: repeated cycles of alcohol use and withdrawal cause progressive upregulation of excitatory NMDA glutamate receptors and downregulation of inhibitory GABA receptors. Over time, this lowers the seizure threshold and increases the risk and severity of complications (seizures, DT) with each withdrawal episode. A patient who had mild tremor during a first withdrawal may develop seizures or DT during subsequent withdrawals. The kindling concept supports the rationale for aggressive treatment of early withdrawal episodes to prevent neurological progression.

stem: CO binds hemoglobin with what relative affinity compared to O2?

choices.feedback: If equal, CO wouldn't be dangerous at low concentrations.
The affinity is much higher — 200-250×.
Much higher than that.

Correct! CO's extreme affinity for Hb means even low concentrations displace significant O2. Additionally, CO shifts the oxyhemoglobin dissociation curve LEFT → remaining O2 is held more tightly → less O2 released to tissues. Double insult: reduced O2 carrying capacity + impaired O2 unloading.

explanation: Carbon monoxide binds hemoglobin with an affinity approximately 200-250 times greater than oxygen, forming carboxyhemoglobin (COHb). This extreme affinity means even small concentrations of CO in inspired air can rapidly displace oxygen from hemoglobin. Beyond simple competitive displacement, CO binding shifts the oxyhemoglobin dissociation curve LEFT, impairing oxygen unloading at the tissues (increased oxygen affinity of remaining unaffected hemoglobin molecules). CO also binds myoglobin and cytochrome oxidase, directly impairing cellular respiration. The combined effect is both reduced oxygen delivery and impaired oxygen utilization.

stem: Why is pulse oximetry unreliable in CO poisoning?

choices.text: Pulse ox only works on arterial blood
choices.feedback: Pulse ox does measure arterial saturation — it just can't distinguish CO-bound Hb.

choices.text: CO interferes with the infrared signal
choices.feedback: The issue is wavelength overlap, not interference.

choices.text: Standard pulse ox cannot distinguish COHb from OxyHb → reads falsely normal
choices.feedback: Correct! Standard pulse oximeters measure light absorption at 2 wavelengths — COHb absorbs similarly to OxyHb at these wavelengths. SpO2 reads normal even with lethal COHb levels. Diagnosis requires CO-OXIMETRY (ABG with co-oximeter) — measures COHb, OxyHb, MetHb separately using multiple wavelengths.

choices.text: CO destroys the pulse oximeter sensor
choices.feedback: The device functions normally; it just misreads.

explanation: Standard pulse oximetry is unreliable in CO poisoning because conventional two-wavelength pulse oximeters cannot distinguish carboxyhemoglobin (COHb) from oxyhemoglobin (OxyHb) — both absorb light at 660 nm similarly. This results in a falsely normal or only slightly reduced SpO2 reading despite severe hypoxemia. A patient with COHb of 40% may display an SpO2 of 97-99%. CO-oximetry (using multiple wavelengths of light) can accurately measure COHb, OxyHb, MetHb, and deoxyhemoglobin separately. ABG with co-oximetry is the definitive test — the PaO2 will be normal (dissolved oxygen is unaffected) but SaO2 measured by co-oximetry will be low.

stem: Multiple family members present with headache and nausea in winter. Suspect:

choices.feedback: While possible, the simultaneous presentation + winter + headache pattern is classic for CO.
Lead is chronic, not acute simultaneous presentation.
Food poisoning typically has more GI symptoms and wouldn't affect pets the same way.

Correct! Classic scenario: multiple household members (including pets) ill with headache, nausea, dizziness — especially in winter with heating systems. CO is odorless and colorless. Ask about furnaces, space heaters, generators. Evacuate immediately. Check co-oximetry. 'Headache improving when leaving home' is pathognomonic.

explanation: Multiple family members presenting simultaneously with headache, nausea, and flu-like symptoms (especially during winter months when heating systems are in use) is the classic presentation of carbon monoxide poisoning from an indoor CO source (malfunctioning furnace, generator, space heater, blocked chimney). The 'whole household sick' pattern is the key diagnostic clue — infectious illness rarely affects all household members simultaneously with identical symptom onset. Pets may also be affected. CO detectors should be checked. Diagnosis is confirmed by co-oximetry showing elevated COHb levels. Treatment is immediate removal from the exposure and high-flow 100% O2.

stem: COHb half-life on 100% O2 via NRB?

choices.feedback: That would be dangerously long.

Correct! Room air: 4-6h. 100% NRB: 60-90 min. Hyperbaric O2: 20-30 min. High-flow 100% O2 is the immediate first-line treatment — start before co-oximetry results. Continue until COHb <3-5% and symptoms resolve.
4-6 hours is the half-life on room air.
20-30 min is the hyperbaric O2 half-life.

explanation: The half-life of carboxyhemoglobin (COHb) on 100% oxygen via non-rebreather mask is approximately 60-90 minutes. This compares to: ~4-6 hours on room air (21% O2), and ~15-30 minutes in a hyperbaric oxygen chamber at 2.5-3 atmospheres. High-flow 100% O2 is the immediate first-line treatment for all CO-poisoned patients — it should be started as soon as CO poisoning is suspected, before COHb levels are available. The non-rebreather mask must be properly fitted with a tight seal and adequate flow rate (15 L/min) to deliver near-100% FiO2.

stem: Indications for hyperbaric oxygen in CO poisoning include:

choices.text: Only if pulse oximetry is low
choices.feedback: Pulse ox is unreliable in CO poisoning — cannot guide treatment decisions.
Mild exposures respond well to 100% NRB alone.

choices.text: LOC, seizures, COHb >25%, cardiac ischemia, pregnancy with significant exposure
choices.feedback: Correct! HBO indications: loss of consciousness/coma, seizures, focal neurologic deficits, COHb >25% (>15% in pregnancy), cardiac ischemia, persistent symptoms despite NRB. HBO reduces COHb half-life to 20-30 min and may reduce delayed neuropsychiatric syndrome risk.
Indications are based on severity, not exposure source.

explanation: Indications for hyperbaric oxygen (HBO) therapy in CO poisoning include: loss of consciousness at any point, seizures, COHb level >25% (some use >20%), cardiac ischemia (troponin elevation, ECG changes), pregnancy (fetal hemoglobin binds CO with even greater affinity), persistent neurological symptoms despite normobaric O2, and severe metabolic acidosis. HBO at 2.5-3 atm reduces COHb half-life to 15-30 minutes, restores cytochrome oxidase function, and may reduce the incidence of delayed neuropsychiatric syndrome. The strongest evidence supports HBO for patients with loss of consciousness. Transfer to an HBO facility should not delay initiation of 100% O2.

stem: Delayed neuropsychiatric syndrome after CO exposure occurs:

choices.feedback: DNS is a delayed phenomenon, not immediate.
Onset is typically within 2-40 days.

Correct! DNS affects 15-40% of significant exposures. Symptoms: memory loss, confusion, personality changes, movement disorders (parkinsonism), depression. May be permanent. Mechanism: delayed demyelination and neuronal apoptosis. HBO therapy may reduce risk. All significant CO exposure patients need cognitive follow-up at 1-2 weeks.
DNS can occur even with moderate exposures that don't cause initial coma.

explanation: Delayed neuropsychiatric syndrome (DNS) occurs 2-40 days after apparent recovery from CO poisoning and affects up to 40% of patients with significant exposure. Symptoms include cognitive impairment (memory, concentration), personality/behavioral changes, parkinsonism, dementia, psychosis, and movement disorders. The mechanism involves CO-mediated lipid peroxidation of myelin in cerebral white matter, delayed immune-mediated demyelination, and apoptosis in vulnerable brain regions (hippocampus, globus pallidus, cerebellum). Hyperbaric oxygen therapy may reduce DNS incidence, though evidence is mixed. MRI may show white matter changes and bilateral globus pallidus lesions.

stem: Cherry-red skin in CO poisoning is:

choices.text: The earliest and most reliable sign
choices.feedback: This is a dangerous misconception.

Correct! Cherry-red skin/lips are classically taught but are actually a LATE finding, often postmortem. Most living CO patients appear normal or pale/cyanotic. Do not wait for or rely on cherry-red skin. Diagnose with co-oximetry. Clinical suspicion based on context (winter, multiple affected, headache) is more important.

choices.text: Present in all cases with COHb >10%
choices.feedback: Many patients with significant COHb never develop cherry-red skin.

choices.text: A sign unique to cyanide poisoning
choices.feedback: Cherry-red is classically associated with both CO and cyanide, but is unreliable for both.

explanation: Cherry-red skin coloration in CO poisoning is a classic but misleading teaching point — it is primarily a late or postmortem finding, rarely observed in living patients. The cherry-red color results from high concentrations of carboxyhemoglobin in skin and mucous membranes. In practice, living CO-poisoned patients more commonly appear normal, pale, or cyanotic. Relying on cherry-red coloration to diagnose CO poisoning would miss the vast majority of cases. The diagnosis should be suspected based on clinical context (exposure history, symptom pattern, multiple patients) and confirmed by co-oximetry, not by skin color assessment.

stem: CO poisoning in pregnancy is especially dangerous because:

choices.text: CO only affects the mother, not the fetus
choices.feedback: The fetus is actually MORE vulnerable than the mother.
CO freely crosses the placenta.
100% O2 is safe and essential in pregnant CO patients.

choices.text: Fetal hemoglobin binds CO with even greater affinity + slower clearance
choices.feedback: Correct! Fetal Hb (HbF) has higher CO affinity than adult Hb → fetal COHb levels are 10-15% higher than maternal levels, and clearance is much slower. Fetal tissue hypoxia occurs at maternal levels that may seem only moderate. Lower threshold for HBO in pregnancy. Consider HBO for any pregnant patient with symptoms or COHb >15%.

explanation: CO poisoning in pregnancy is especially dangerous because fetal hemoglobin (HbF) binds CO with 10-15% greater affinity than adult hemoglobin, resulting in higher fetal COHb levels than maternal levels. Additionally, fetal COHb elimination is slower because it depends on maternal-fetal oxygen gradient across the placenta. Fetal tissue oxygen delivery is already marginal under normal conditions — even modest COHb elevations can cause fetal hypoxia, leading to neurological damage, growth restriction, or fetal death. Pregnant patients should receive high-flow 100% O2 for longer durations, and HBO therapy is recommended at lower COHb thresholds than for non-pregnant adults.

stem: Classic opioid overdose triad?

choices.feedback: These can occur but aren't the classic triad.
That suggests serotonin syndrome or sympathomimetic.

Correct! The triad is pathognomonic. Respiratory depression is the lethal mechanism — opioids suppress the brainstem respiratory drive (mu receptors). Pinpoint pupils (miosis) is characteristic but not universal (exceptions: meperidine, severe hypoxia, co-ingestions).
That's a sympathomimetic toxidrome.

explanation: The classic opioid overdose triad is: CNS depression (altered consciousness, ranging from drowsiness to coma), respiratory depression (decreased rate and depth, the primary cause of death), and miosis (pinpoint pupils from stimulation of the Edinger-Westphal nucleus). Respiratory depression is the mechanism of death — opioids suppress medullary respiratory centers, reducing both the ventilatory drive and response to hypercapnia/hypoxia. Miosis is present in most opioid overdoses but can be absent with co-ingestion of sympathomimetics, with meperidine (which has anticholinergic properties), or in the setting of severe hypoxia/anoxic brain injury (mydriasis).

stem: Naloxone should be titrated to:

choices.feedback: Full reversal in dependent patients precipitates withdrawal and is unnecessary.
Pupil size is not the target of naloxone titration.

Correct! Goal: reverse life-threatening respiratory depression without precipitating full withdrawal. Start low, titrate up. Opioid-dependent patients: use smaller initial doses. Complete reversal causes acute withdrawal (non-lethal but causes agitation, vomiting, aspiration risk).
Naloxone shouldn't be titrated to pain endpoints in overdose.

explanation: Naloxone should be titrated to adequate respiratory effort — NOT to full consciousness. The goal is to restore spontaneous ventilation (respiratory rate >12, adequate tidal volume, SpO2 >92%) while avoiding precipitating acute opioid withdrawal. Full reversal in opioid-dependent patients triggers severe withdrawal (agitation, vomiting with aspiration risk, hypertension, tachycardia, and rarely pulmonary edema). Start with low doses (0.04-0.4 mg IV) in suspected opioid-dependent patients, escalating as needed. Higher initial doses (2 mg) are appropriate when dependency is unlikely or when respiratory arrest is imminent.

stem: Why is re-sedation a major concern after naloxone?

choices.feedback: Naloxone is a pure antagonist with no sedative effects.
Tolerance to naloxone doesn't develop with single doses.
Re-sedation can occur with any opioid, especially long-acting agents.

Correct! Most opioids last longer than naloxone → as naloxone wears off, opioid effects return → re-sedation with respiratory depression. Especially dangerous with: methadone (half-life 24-36h), extended-release formulations, fentanyl patches. Observe ≥4h (short-acting) to ≥24h (long-acting). Consider naloxone infusion.

explanation: Re-sedation after naloxone is a major concern because naloxone has a shorter duration of action (30-90 minutes) than most opioids. Long-acting opioids such as methadone (half-life 15-60 hours), extended-release formulations, and fentanyl patches can continue to release drug long after naloxone wears off. Patients who respond to naloxone must be monitored for at minimum 4-6 hours (longer for long-acting opioids). Repeated naloxone boluses or a continuous naloxone infusion (two-thirds of the effective bolus dose per hour) may be needed. Patients should never be discharged immediately after naloxone administration.

stem: Methadone overdose has unique risks including:

choices.feedback: Correct! Methadone uniquely prolongs QTc (blocks hERG K+ channels) → risk of torsades de pointes. Also: very long half-life (24-36h) means toxicity outlasts naloxone → need extended observation (≥24h) and possible naloxone infusion. Unlike most opioids, methadone requires cardiac monitoring.
Blindness is associated with methanol poisoning.
Hepatotoxicity is more associated with acetaminophen.

choices.text: Seizures as the primary lethal mechanism
choices.feedback: Respiratory depression remains the primary lethal mechanism. Meperidine is the opioid most associated with seizures.

explanation: Methadone carries unique risks beyond standard opioid toxicity, most importantly QTc prolongation with risk of torsades de pointes. Methadone blocks the hERG (human ether-à-go-go-related gene) potassium channel, prolonging cardiac repolarization. This risk is dose-dependent but can occur at therapeutic doses, especially with concurrent QTc-prolonging medications. Other unique methadone risks include extremely long and variable half-life (15-60 hours, necessitating prolonged monitoring after overdose), accumulation during dose titration, and drug interactions with CYP3A4 and CYP2B6 inhibitors. Baseline and follow-up ECGs are recommended during methadone maintenance.

stem: If a patient doesn't respond to 10 mg naloxone total, you should:

choices.feedback: Correct! No response to 10 mg IV naloxone → strongly reconsider diagnosis. Consider: co-ingestions (benzodiazepines, GHB, barbiturates), non-opioid causes of CNS depression (stroke, hypoglycemia, head trauma, sepsis), polysubstance use. Exception: massive fentanyl exposure may require higher doses.
If 10 mg total doesn't work, opioids are unlikely the sole cause.
Charcoal is unlikely to help at this point and is dangerous in an obtunded patient without airway protection.
Flumazenil should be used cautiously and only if benzodiazepine overdose is suspected.

explanation:
Correct Answer: Reconsider the diagnosis

Reconsider the diagnosis (may not be opioid overdose).

- Give 20 mg more naloxone: Does not align with the clinical presentation and pathophysiology described in this case.
- Administer activated charcoal: Does not align with the clinical presentation and pathophysiology described in this case.

stem: Opioid withdrawal is distinguished from alcohol/benzo withdrawal by:

choices.feedback: Opioid withdrawal is extremely uncomfortable but not life-threatening.
The clinical presentations and risks differ significantly.

Correct! Opioid withdrawal: dysphoria, myalgias, diarrhea, vomiting, piloerection, yawning, lacrimation, rhinorrhea. Extremely uncomfortable but NOT fatal. Alcohol and benzodiazepine withdrawal CAN kill (seizures, delirium tremens → autonomic instability → death). This distinction is critical for management.

choices.text: Opioid withdrawal causes seizures while alcohol doesn't
choices.feedback: It's the opposite — alcohol/benzo withdrawal causes seizures.

explanation: Opioid withdrawal, while extremely uncomfortable, is NOT life-threatening in healthy adults — this distinguishes it from alcohol and benzodiazepine withdrawal, which can cause fatal seizures and delirium tremens. Opioid withdrawal symptoms include lacrimation, rhinorrhea, yawning, piloerection (goosebumps), diaphoresis, myalgias, nausea/vomiting, diarrhea, abdominal cramping, anxiety, and insomnia. The main risk of opioid withdrawal is dehydration from vomiting/diarrhea (particularly dangerous in neonates and debilitated patients) and the high risk of relapse and subsequent overdose (due to lost tolerance). Treatment includes symptom management (clonidine, loperamide, NSAIDs) or opioid agonist therapy (buprenorphine, methadone).

stem: Non-cardiogenic pulmonary edema in opioid overdose is most associated with:

choices.feedback: Tramadol is more associated with seizures and serotonin syndrome than NCPE.
Any opioid can cause it but heroin is the classic association.

Correct! Heroin is classically associated with non-cardiogenic pulmonary edema (NCPE). Mechanism: acute alveolar-capillary membrane damage + negative-pressure pulmonary edema from airway obstruction. Can occur even after successful naloxone reversal. Treatment: supportive (oxygen, NIV/intubation if needed). Usually resolves within 24-48h.
While possible, NCPE is most classically linked to heroin/methadone.

explanation: Non-cardiogenic pulmonary edema (NCPE) in opioid overdose is most commonly associated with heroin and methadone. The mechanism is multifactorial: opioid-induced pulmonary capillary permeability increase, negative-pressure pulmonary edema from inspiratory effort against a closed glottis, and neurogenic pulmonary edema from CNS effects. NCPE can develop acutely during overdose or after naloxone administration (controversial association). Chest X-ray shows bilateral fluffy infiltrates without cardiomegaly. Treatment is supportive — supplemental oxygen, CPAP/BiPAP, or mechanical ventilation as needed. It typically resolves within 24-48 hours with supportive care.

stem: Intranasal naloxone dose for bystander administration?

choices.feedback: That's an IV dose, not intranasal.

Correct! naloxone (Narcan) nasal spray: 4 mg into one nostril, may repeat after 2-3 min in alternating nostrils. Available OTC for community/bystander use. Slightly slower onset than IV (~3-5 min vs 1-2 min) but no needles required. Key public health intervention for fentanyl crisis. All patients should receive rescue naloxone prescription at opioid prescribing.
0.04 mg is a low IV starting dose for opioid-dependent patients.
40 mg is far too much and not a standard dosing.

Correct Answer: 4 mg per nostril

stem: DUMBBELSS mnemonic: the most life-threatening muscarinic effect?

choices.feedback: Correct! The 'B's in DUMBBELSS are the killers: Bronchospasm (airway constriction) + Bronchorrhea (massive secretion production — can produce liters). Combined with respiratory muscle weakness (nicotinic) and central respiratory depression (CNS) → respiratory failure = #1 cause of death. This is why atropine endpoint is drying of secretions.
Incontinence is distressing but not life-threatening.
Diarrhea is uncomfortable but not immediately lethal.
Tearing is a minor symptom.

explanation: Best answer: Bronchospasm + bronchorrhea (secretion flooding of airways).

stem: Atropine treats which receptor effects in OP poisoning?

choices.feedback: Correct! Atropine = competitive muscarinic antagonist. Treats: bronchorrhea, bronchospasm, bradycardia, salivation, miosis, GI/GU hypermotility. Does NOT treat: fasciculations, muscle weakness, paralysis (nicotinic — need pralidoxime). Does NOT treat CNS effects (need benzodiazepines for seizures).
Atropine only blocks muscarinic receptors.
Atropine has NO nicotinic effect — pralidoxime treats nicotinic symptoms.
Atropine has limited CNS penetration.

explanation: Toxicology management follows the systematic approach: stabilize ABCs, identify the toxidrome, decontaminate when appropriate, administer specific antidote if available, and provide supportive care. Always contact Poison Control (1-800-222-1222) for guidance.

stem: Pralidoxime (2-PAM) works by:

choices.feedback: That's atropine's mechanism.

choices.text: Reactivating acetylcholinesterase by cleaving the OP-enzyme bond
choices.feedback: Correct! 2-PAM is a nucleophilic oxime that attacks the OP-AChE bond → frees the enzyme → restores AChE activity. Must be given BEFORE 'aging' (irreversible conformational change of the OP-enzyme complex). Aging time varies: sarin ~5h, VX ~40h, some pesticides longer. Treats nicotinic effects (weakness, fasciculations).
2-PAM doesn't destroy the OP — it separates it from the enzyme.
That's the mechanism of benzodiazepines.

stem: Atropine dosing endpoint in OP poisoning:

choices.feedback: Pupil size is an unreliable endpoint.
Tachycardia is an unreliable endpoint and may be from nicotinic stimulation.
BP normalization is not the atropine endpoint.

Correct! Clear the lungs — bronchorrhea is the lethal muscarinic effect. Pupil dilation and tachycardia are poor endpoints (may be caused by nicotinic effects or fear). Dose: 2-4 mg IV, double q5 min until secretions clear. Massive doses may be needed (10-100+ mg). Do NOT under-dose atropine.

stem: Difference between organophosphate and carbamate poisoning?

choices.feedback: Both cause cholinergic crisis (muscarinic + nicotinic).

choices.text: Carbamates are not found in pesticides
choices.feedback: Many pesticides contain carbamates (carbaryl, aldicarb).
Both cause miosis via muscarinic stimulation.

Correct! Carbamates reversibly bind AChE → enzyme spontaneously reactivates over hours. OPs irreversibly bind → require pralidoxime before aging. Clinical implication: carbamate poisoning is shorter duration, less severe CNS effects, and pralidoxime is generally not needed. Atropine is still first-line for both.

explanation: Key detail: reversible (spontaneous recovery).

stem: Intermediate syndrome occurs:

choices.feedback: That timeline suggests delayed polyneuropathy (OPIDN), a different complication.
Immediate effects are the cholinergic crisis.

Correct! Intermediate syndrome: 24-96h after initial cholinergic crisis resolves. Proximal muscle weakness, neck flexor weakness, cranial nerve palsies, respiratory muscle weakness → may need ventilation. Due to persistent NMJ dysfunction (nicotinic receptor desensitization). Can be fatal if respiratory support not provided.

choices.text: Only with nerve agents, not pesticides
choices.feedback: Intermediate syndrome occurs with both pesticides and nerve agents.

explanation: Best answer: 24-96 hours after exposure: proximal weakness and respiratory failure.

stem: Healthcare worker exposure risk in OP poisoning:

choices.text: No risk — OPs only enter through ingestion
choices.feedback: OPs can be absorbed through skin and inhaled.

choices.text: Significant — OPs absorb through skin; PPE is essential before decontamination
choices.feedback: Correct! OPs (especially nerve agents and concentrated pesticides) can be absorbed through skin, inhaled, and through mucous membranes. Healthcare workers MUST wear full PPE (impervious gowns, gloves, respiratory protection) before contact. Remove patient's clothing (removes ~80% of contamination) + copious soap/water decontamination. Secondary contamination of healthcare workers is well-documented.

choices.text: Risk is limited to the first 5 minutes
choices.feedback: Contaminated clothing and skin can off-gas and expose others for extended periods.

choices.text: Risk only from nerve agents, not pesticides
choices.feedback: Both nerve agents and concentrated pesticides pose dermal absorption risk.

explanation: Toxicology management follows the systematic approach: stabilize ABCs, identify the toxidrome, decontaminate when appropriate, administer specific antidote if available, and provide supportive care. Always contact Poison Control (1-800-222-1222) for guidance.

stem: A farmer presents with miosis, excessive salivation, muscle fasciculations, and garlic odor. Treatment order?

choices.feedback: Naloxone is for opioids. This presentation is cholinergic, not opioid.
Flumazenil is for benzodiazepines. Charcoal has limited role.
Wrong order — decontamination and atropine are more urgent.

choices.text: PPE + decontamination → atropine (for muscarinic) → pralidoxime (for nicotinic) → benzos if seizures
choices.feedback: Correct! Sequence: (1) PPE for providers + patient decontamination, (2) ABCs/airway, (3) Atropinedoubled q5 min until secretions dry, (4) Pralidoxime(before aging), (5) Benzodiazepines for seizures. The garlic odor + agricultural setting + cholinergic symptoms = classic OP presentation.

explanation: Best answer: PPE + decontamination → atropine (for muscarinic) → pralidoxime (for nicotinic) → benzos if seizures.

stem: Earliest symptom of salicylate toxicity?

choices.feedback: Seizures are a LATE and severe finding.
Renal involvement is a late complication.
Hyperthermia occurs later from uncoupled oxidative phosphorylation.

Correct! Tinnitus (ringing in the ears) is the earliest and most reliable symptom of salicylate toxicity. Occurs at relatively low levels (~30 mg/dL). Followed by nausea, vomiting, hyperventilation. Late: confusion, seizures, coma. Tinnitus in a patient with unexplained acid-base disturbance = check salicylate level.

explanation: Tinnitus (ringing in the ears) is typically the earliest symptom of salicylate toxicity, occurring at serum levels of 20-40 mg/dL. It results from direct ototoxic effects on cochlear hair cells (increased sensitivity and spontaneous discharge). Other early symptoms include nausea, vomiting, and diaphoresis. As toxicity progresses: tachypnea (respiratory alkalosis from direct stimulation of the medullary respiratory center), altered mental status, hyperthermia, seizures, and eventually cardiovascular collapse. Tinnitus in the setting of aspirin use is an important clinical clue that levels may be supratherapeutic.

stem: Classic acid-base disturbance in salicylate poisoning?

choices.feedback: Correct! Pathognomonic pattern: early respiratory alkalosis (direct medullary stimulation → hyperventilation) + late HAGMA (lactic acid, ketoacids from uncoupled oxidative phosphorylation). The mixed pattern on ABG should always prompt checking a salicylate level. Children may present with predominant HAGMA.
Significant salicylate poisoning always disrupts acid-base balance.
Metabolic alkalosis is not a feature of salicylate toxicity.
Salicylates cause hyperventilation (respiratory alkalosis), not hypoventilation.

explanation: Salicylate poisoning produces a characteristic mixed acid-base disturbance: primary respiratory alkalosis (from direct stimulation of the medullary respiratory center causing hyperventilation) combined with primary metabolic acidosis (from uncoupling of oxidative phosphorylation, inhibition of Krebs cycle enzymes, and accumulation of organic acids). In adults, respiratory alkalosis typically predominates early. In children, metabolic acidosis predominates earlier due to less respiratory reserve. The arterial pH can be alkalemic, normal, or acidemic depending on the stage — a normal pH with a low bicarbonate and low PaCO2 is a red flag for impending decompensation.

stem: Why is sodium bicarbonate the key treatment?

choices.text: Alkalinization traps ionized salicylate in blood
choices.feedback: Correct! Ion trapping: alkaline pH → salicylate ionized → cannot cross BBB (reduces CNS toxicity) and cannot be reabsorbed in renal tubules (enhances urinary excretion). Goal pH: serum 7.45-7.55, urine 7.5-8.0. Must correct hypokalemia first — kidneys cannot alkalinize urine when K is low.
The respiratory alkalosis is actually protective — don't suppress it.

choices.text: It neutralizes aspirin in the stomach
choices.feedback: Bicarb works systemically, not as a GI antacid.
Bicarb doesn't bind salicylate — it changes pH to trap the ionized form.

explanation: Sodium bicarbonate is the cornerstone of salicylate poisoning treatment because urinary alkalinization (target urine pH 7.5-8.0) traps ionized salicylate in the renal tubules, dramatically enhancing urinary excretion. Salicylic acid (weak acid, pKa ~3.0) exists in ionized form at alkaline pH — ionized molecules cannot cross cell membranes, so they are 'trapped' in the alkaline urine and excreted. Simultaneously, serum alkalinization reduces CNS penetration of salicylate (the same ion-trapping principle at the blood-brain barrier). Even a small decrease in blood pH dramatically increases CNS salicylate concentration — maintaining serum alkalemia is critical.

stem: Why is intubation DANGEROUS in salicylate poisoning?

choices.feedback: Laryngospasm is not a feature of salicylate toxicity.

choices.text: Loss of hyperventilation → CO2 rises → acidemia → increased brain salicylate → cardiac arrest
choices.feedback: Correct! The patient's hyperventilation is a critical compensatory mechanism maintaining alkaline pH. Intubation + sedation → loss of this compensation → rapid CO2 accumulation → acute acidemia → un-ionized salicylate floods CNS → seizures, cerebral edema, cardiac arrest. Many salicylate deaths occur peri-intubation. If must intubate: pre-bolus bicarb, match high minute ventilation, emergency HD.
While salicylate can cause pulmonary edema, that's not the main intubation risk.
Drug interaction is not the concern.

explanation: Intubation in salicylate poisoning is extremely dangerous because it removes the patient's compensatory hyperventilation. Salicylate-poisoned patients hyperventilate to maintain respiratory alkalosis, which keeps salicylate ionized and prevents CNS penetration. If intubated, the ventilator typically cannot match the patient's pre-intubation minute ventilation (which may be 2-3× normal). CO2 rises, pH drops, and the resulting acidemia shifts un-ionized salicylate across the blood-brain barrier into the CNS — causing rapid clinical deterioration, seizures, and death. If intubation is absolutely necessary, it must be performed with the highest possible respiratory rate and tidal volume to maintain alkalemia.

stem: Hypokalemia must be corrected in salicylate poisoning because:

choices.text: Kidneys cannot alkalinize urine when potassium is low
choices.feedback: Correct! In hypokalemia, kidneys reabsorb K+ and excrete H+ to compensate → urine becomes acidic → bicarb therapy fails (can't trap salicylate in urine). Must replete K aggressively for alkalinization to work. Target K >4.0. This is a frequently tested concept.
While true, this isn't the primary concern in this context.

choices.text: Potassium is needed for activated charcoal to work
choices.feedback: Charcoal mechanism is unrelated to potassium.
Potassium doesn't affect salicylate absorption.

explanation: Hypokalemia must be corrected in salicylate poisoning because the kidneys cannot effectively alkalinize the urine when potassium is depleted. In hypokalemia, the renal tubules reabsorb potassium in exchange for hydrogen ions — the kidneys excrete H+ to conserve K+, producing paradoxically acidic urine despite sodium bicarbonate infusion. This defeats the therapeutic goal of urinary alkalinization. Aggressive potassium replacement (targeting K+ >4.0 mEq/L) is essential to allow bicarbonate therapy to achieve adequate urinary pH. Volume resuscitation with potassium-containing fluids should be initiated early.

stem: Indications for hemodialysis in salicylate poisoning:

choices.feedback: HD is indicated for severe toxicity regardless of acute or chronic ingestion.

choices.text: Only after 24 hours of bicarb therapy
choices.feedback: Don't wait — HD should be initiated promptly when indicated.

choices.text: Level >100, renal failure, AMS, pulmonary edema, acidemia (pH <7.2), clinical deterioration
choices.feedback: Correct! HD directly removes salicylate from blood AND corrects acid-base disturbances. Definitive treatment for severe poisoning. Don't delay HD for bicarb to 'work' — if HD criteria met, initiate immediately. Also indicated: failure to respond to aggressive bicarb therapy, need for intubation (relative indication).
30 mg/dL is mild toxicity — bicarb is sufficient.

explanation: Indications for hemodialysis in salicylate poisoning include: serum salicylate level >100 mg/dL, altered mental status, renal failure (inability to renally eliminate salicylate), pulmonary edema (ARDS from salicylate-induced capillary leak), severe acidemia (pH <7.2 despite bicarbonate therapy), and clinical deterioration despite aggressive medical management. Hemodialysis rapidly removes salicylate and corrects acid-base disturbances. It is the definitive treatment for severe poisoning. Early nephrology consultation is recommended when levels approach 80-90 mg/dL or when there is any evidence of end-organ toxicity.

stem: Multi-dose activated charcoal is uniquely useful in salicylate poisoning because:

choices.feedback: MDAC use is based on clinical pharmacology, not regulation.

choices.text: Charcoal prevents vomiting in salicylate poisoning
choices.feedback: Charcoal doesn't prevent vomiting — salicylates commonly cause nausea.

choices.text: Salicylates are not absorbed by charcoal on first dose
choices.feedback: Charcoal does absorb salicylates on first dose.

Correct! Salicylates (especially enteric-coated or large ingestions) form concretions/bezoars in the stomach → continued absorption over hours. Multi-dose charcoal (MDAC) can adsorb salicylate still in the GI tract AND may enhance elimination through 'gut dialysis' (salicylate excreted into the GI lumen is re-adsorbed by charcoal). Whole bowel irrigation also considered for large ingestions.

explanation: Multi-dose activated charcoal (MDAC) is uniquely useful in salicylate poisoning because salicylates can form bezoars/concretions in the stomach (particularly enteric-coated formulations), leading to prolonged and erratic absorption. MDAC (50 g every 4 hours) can bind residual salicylate in the GI tract. Additionally, salicylates undergo enterohepatic recirculation — MDAC can interrupt this cycle by binding salicylate secreted into the bile. Salicylate absorption can continue for many hours post-ingestion with large or enteric-coated doses, so serial salicylate levels are essential (do not rely on a single level to exclude significant ingestion).

stem: Chronic salicylate toxicity compared to acute:

choices.feedback: Chronic toxicity still causes acid-base disturbance, often HAGMA predominant.

choices.text: Only occurs with topical salicylate products
choices.feedback: Chronic toxicity occurs with oral aspirin and other salicylate formulations.
Chronic toxicity can be more dangerous.

Correct! Chronic toxicity is often more severe because: (1) salicylate accumulates gradually → more time for CNS penetration, (2) patients are often elderly on therapeutic doses → diagnosis delayed (misattributed to other conditions), (3) presents with AMS/confusion without classic acute findings, (4) higher mortality than acute. Always check salicylate levels in elderly patients with unexplained altered mental status + acid-base disturbance.

explanation: Chronic salicylate toxicity is often more dangerous than acute overdose because: it is frequently unrecognized (presenting as altered mental status, tachypnea, or metabolic derangements in elderly patients), diagnosis is delayed (not suspected in non-overdose settings), and total body salicylate burden is higher due to saturable elimination kinetics (at steady-state toxic levels, small dose increases cause disproportionate level elevations). Chronic toxicity occurs at lower serum levels than acute toxicity because tissue distribution has reached equilibrium — CNS levels are proportionally higher. Mortality from chronic salicylate toxicity (up to 25%) exceeds that from acute intentional overdose.

stem: Methanol toxicity primarily damages which organ?

choices.feedback: Cardiac toxicity is not a primary feature of methanol poisoning.

Correct! Formic acid (methanol metabolite) is selectively toxic to retinal ganglion cells and optic nerve. Symptoms: blurred vision, 'snowstorm' visual field, central scotoma, blindness. Optic disc hyperemia on fundoscopy. Visual complaints in a patient with HAGMA = methanol until proven otherwise.
Hepatotoxicity is more characteristic of acetaminophen.
Kidney damage is characteristic of ethylene glycol (calcium oxalate crystals).

explanation: Methanol toxicity primarily damages the eyes through its toxic metabolite formic acid (formate). Formic acid inhibits mitochondrial cytochrome oxidase in the retina and optic nerve, causing retinal edema, optic disc hyperemia, and ultimately optic nerve atrophy and blindness. Visual symptoms include blurred vision, central scotomata, decreased visual acuity, and 'snowfield vision.' The putamen/basal ganglia are also selectively vulnerable, potentially causing parkinsonian features. There is a latent period of 12-24 hours between ingestion and symptom onset (while alcohol dehydrogenase converts methanol to formaldehyde, then formaldehyde dehydrogenase converts it to formic acid).

stem: Calcium oxalate crystals in urine suggest poisoning with:

choices.feedback: Isopropyl produces acetone, not oxalate.

Correct! Ethylene glycol → glycolic acid → oxalic acid → combines with calcium → calcium oxalate crystals deposited in renal tubules (AKI) and found in urine (envelope-shaped/monohydrate or needle-shaped/dihydrate). Also: Wood lamp fluorescence of urine (antifreeze contains fluorescein).
Methanol produces formic acid, not oxalate.
Ethanol doesn't produce oxalate crystals.

explanation: Calcium oxalate crystals (envelope-shaped under polarized light) in the urine are pathognomonic for ethylene glycol poisoning. Ethylene glycol is metabolized by alcohol dehydrogenase to glycoaldehyde → glycolic acid → glyoxylic acid → oxalic acid. Oxalic acid combines with calcium to form calcium oxalate crystals that deposit in renal tubules, causing acute kidney injury. Other EG poisoning features include initial CNS depression (appearing 'drunk'), followed by cardiopulmonary compromise (12-24 hours), then renal failure (24-72 hours). The initial metabolite glycolic acid is primarily responsible for the anion gap acidosis.

stem: First-line antidote for methanol/ethylene glycol?

choices.feedback: NAC is for acetaminophen, not toxic alcohols.
Alcohols are rapidly absorbed; charcoal is ineffective.
Ethanol works but is harder to dose and has more side effects. It's the alternative, not first-line.

Correct! Fomepizole inhibits alcohol dehydrogenase → blocks formation of toxic metabolites. Give early (before metabolites accumulate). Loading weight-based dosing IV, then weight-based dosing q12h. Preferred over ethanol: predictable pharmacokinetics, fewer side effects, no need for level monitoring.

explanation: Fomepizole (4-methylpyrazole) is the first-line antidote for methanol and ethylene glycol poisoning. It competitively inhibits alcohol dehydrogenase, blocking the conversion of the parent alcohol to its toxic metabolites (formic acid from methanol; glycolic/oxalic acid from ethylene glycol). Fomepizole is preferred over ethanol because it has predictable pharmacokinetics, does not cause CNS depression, does not require ICU-level monitoring for titration, and has fewer side effects. IV ethanol is an acceptable alternative when fomepizole is unavailable. Fomepizole dosing must be adjusted during hemodialysis as it is dialyzable.

stem: Isopropyl alcohol is distinguished from methanol/EG by:

choices.feedback: Isopropyl is generally less dangerous than methanol/EG.

choices.text: Osmolar gap WITHOUT anion gap acidosis
choices.feedback: Correct! Isopropyl alcohol → acetone (a ketone, not an acid). Creates osmolar gap and positive ketones but NO metabolic acidosis. Methanol/EG both cause HAGMA. Ketones without acidosis = isopropyl. Treatment is supportive (no fomepizole needed).
Oxalate crystals are from ethylene glycol.
Blindness is from methanol (formic acid retinal toxicity).

explanation: Isopropyl alcohol (isopropanol/rubbing alcohol) is distinguished from methanol and ethylene glycol by producing an osmolar gap WITHOUT an anion gap metabolic acidosis. Isopropanol is metabolized to acetone (a ketone, not an acid), so it causes ketonemia and ketonuria without acidosis. The urine ketone dipstick will be positive (nitroprusside reacts with acetone), and the patient's breath may smell fruity. In contrast, methanol and ethylene glycol produce severe anion gap metabolic acidosis through their toxic acid metabolites. Isopropanol is the least toxic of the three common toxic alcohols and rarely requires hemodialysis.

stem: Indications for hemodialysis in toxic alcohol ingestion include:

choices.feedback: HD is indicated based on severity criteria regardless of fomepizole availability.
Detectable levels alone don't mandate dialysis.

choices.text: Severe acidosis (pH <7.25), renal failure, visual changes, level >50 mg/dL
choices.feedback: Correct! HD removes both parent alcohol and toxic metabolites. Indicated for: pH <7.25 (severe acidosis), end-organ damage (visual changes in methanol, AKI in EG), alcohol level >50 mg/dL, deterioration despite fomepizole. Fomepizole alone may suffice if caught early with no significant acidosis.
Osmolar gap alone without acidosis or end-organ damage doesn't mandate HD.

explanation: Indications for hemodialysis in toxic alcohol poisoning include: severe metabolic acidosis (pH <7.25), renal failure, visual disturbance in methanol poisoning, significant end-organ damage, serum methanol or ethylene glycol level >50 mg/dL, and deterioration despite appropriate antidote therapy. Hemodialysis removes both the parent alcohol and its toxic metabolites, corrects the acidosis, and corrects electrolyte abnormalities. It is the definitive treatment for severe poisoning. During dialysis, fomepizole dosing must be increased (it is dialyzable) or ethanol infusion rate increased to maintain therapeutic levels.

stem: Early toxic alcohol ingestion shows high osmolar gap. As metabolism continues:

choices.text: Osmolar gap increases while anion gap decreases
choices.feedback: It's the opposite — osmolar gap decreases as the parent is metabolized.
The gaps evolve as parent compound is metabolized.

choices.text: Osmolar gap decreases while anion gap increases
choices.feedback: Correct! Early: parent compound (osmotically active) → high osmolar gap, minimal acidosis. As ADH metabolizes parent → toxic acid metabolites: osmolar gap falls (parent cleared) while anion gap rises (acid accumulates). Late presentation may have normal osmolar gap but severe HAGMA — don't be falsely reassured by normal osmolar gap.
They move in opposite directions.

explanation: The evolution of toxic alcohol poisoning shows a characteristic reciprocal relationship between the osmolar gap and anion gap. Early after ingestion, the parent alcohol (methanol or ethylene glycol) is present in high concentrations, causing a high osmolar gap. As alcohol dehydrogenase converts the parent alcohol to its toxic acid metabolites, the osmolar gap decreases while the anion gap increases. Late presentations may show a normal osmolar gap with a severely elevated anion gap — the parent alcohol has been fully metabolized. This 'osmolar gap to anion gap transition' is diagnostically important: a normal osmolar gap does NOT exclude toxic alcohol poisoning.

stem: Folate is given adjunctively in methanol poisoning because:

choices.feedback: Folate doesn't reverse established retinal damage.

choices.text: It enhances formate metabolism to CO2 and water
choices.feedback: Correct! Folate (leucovorin or folic acid) is a cofactor for the enzyme that converts formic acid → CO2 + H2O. Enhances elimination of the toxic metabolite. Give IV q4-6h. Analogous: thiamine + pyridoxine in EG poisoning shunt metabolism away from oxalate.
Fomepizole blocks ADH. Folate enhances downstream metabolism.
Renal protection is relevant to EG, not methanol.

explanation: Folate (leucovorin or folinic acid) is given adjunctively in methanol poisoning because it serves as a cofactor for the enzyme that converts formic acid (formate) to carbon dioxide and water via the tetrahydrofolate pathway. By enhancing formate metabolism, folate helps reduce the accumulation of the toxic metabolite responsible for optic nerve damage and metabolic acidosis. This is analogous to how pyridoxine and thiamine are given adjunctively in ethylene glycol poisoning to promote metabolism of glyoxylic acid to nontoxic products (glycine via pyridoxine, alpha-hydroxy-beta-ketoadipate via thiamine).

stem: Sweet-tasting antifreeze ingestion causing AKI in a child. Diagnosis?

choices.feedback: Methanol (windshield fluid) is not typically sweet-tasting.
Isopropyl (rubbing alcohol) doesn't cause AKI and has a bitter taste.
APAP causes hepatotoxicity, not AKI from crystal deposition.

Correct! Ethylene glycol (antifreeze) has a notoriously sweet taste → accidental ingestion in children and pets. Metabolized to glycolic/oxalic acid → calcium oxalate crystals → renal tubular obstruction → AKI. Treatment: fomepizole + HD if severe. Prevention: bitter additives now added to some antifreeze products.

explanation: Sweet-tasting antifreeze ingestion causing acute kidney injury in a child is the classic presentation of ethylene glycol poisoning. Ethylene glycol (the main component of automotive antifreeze) has a characteristically sweet taste, making accidental pediatric ingestion common. The metabolic pathway produces glycolic acid (causing anion gap metabolic acidosis) and oxalic acid (which precipitates as calcium oxalate crystals in renal tubules, causing AKI). Other clues include fluorescence of the urine under Wood's lamp (some antifreeze contains fluorescein), hypocalcemia (from calcium binding to oxalate), and cranial nerve palsies. Treatment: fomepizole and possible hemodialysis.

stem: Sympathomimetic and anticholinergic toxidromes are distinguished by:

choices.feedback: Both can cause agitation and altered mental status.
Both cause tachycardia.
Both have mydriasis (dilated pupils).

Correct! Both: tachycardia, hypertension, mydriasis, agitation. Key difference: sympathomimetic = DIAPHORETIC (wet/sweating). Anticholinergic = DRY ('dry as a bone'). This is the single most reliable distinguishing feature and extremely high-yield.

explanation: Correct: Skin moisture (diaphoretic vs dry).

stem: Pinpoint pupils + respiratory depression + CNS depression =

choices.feedback: Sympathomimetic has dilated pupils, tachycardia, hypertension.
Anticholinergic has DILATED pupils and dry skin.
Serotonin syndrome has clonus, hyperreflexia, hyperthermia, mydriasis.

Correct! Classic opioid triad: miosis (pinpoint pupils) + respiratory depression + CNS depression. Treat with naloxone (0.4-IV, titrate to respiratory effort). Distinguish from sedative-hypnotic (normal/mildly small pupils, no miosis).

explanation: Toxicology management follows the systematic approach: stabilize ABCs, identify the toxidrome, decontaminate when appropriate, administer specific antidote if available, and provide supportive care. Always contact Poison Control (1-800-222-1222) for guidance.

stem: DUMBBELSS mnemonic describes which toxidrome?

choices.feedback: Sympathomimetic causes the opposite of most DUMBBELSS features.

Correct! DUMBBELSS: Diarrhea, Urination, Miosis, Bronchospasm, Bradycardia, Emesis, Lacrimation, Salivation, Sweating. Caused by: organophosphates, carbamates, nerve agents, excess pyridostigmine. Treatment: atropine (muscarinic antagonist) + pralidoxime (reactivates acetylcholinesterase).
Anticholinergic is the OPPOSITE (dry, not DUMBBELSS).
Opioid toxidrome: miosis + CNS/respiratory depression. No DUMBBELSS.

explanation: Correct: Cholinergic (muscarinic excess).

stem: Antidote for TCA overdose?

choices.feedback: Atropine treats cholinergic toxicity, not anticholinergic/TCA.
Flumazenil reverses benzodiazepines. Contraindicated in TCA overdose (seizure risk).

Correct! TCA overdose: wide QRS (>100ms), seizures, hypotension, anticholinergic effects. Sodium bicarb: alkalinizes blood → increases protein binding of TCA, overcomes sodium channel blockade → narrows QRS. Give bolus 1-2 mEq/kg IV for QRS >100ms. Target pH 7.50-7.55.
NAC is the antidote for acetaminophen.

explanation: TCA overdose causes a dangerous triad: (1) cardiac toxicity (QRS widening → sodium channel blockade, QT prolongation → ventricular arrhythmias), (2) anticholinergic syndrome (mydriasis, dry skin, urinary retention, tachycardia, hyperthermia), (3) CNS toxicity (seizures, altered mental status). Sodium bicarbonate is the antidote for QRS >100ms — it provides sodium to overcome channel blockade and alkalinization to reduce free drug. Target serum pH 7.45-7.55.

stem: Antidote for β-blocker overdose?

choices.feedback: Fab fragments are for digoxin toxicity.

Correct! Glucagon bypasses the beta-receptor → activates adenylyl cyclase directly → increases cAMP → positive inotropy and chronotropy. Dose: 3-IV bolus, then infusion. For beta-blocker overdose causing severe bradycardia and hypotension refractory to atropine. Also: high-dose insulin for refractory cases.
Calcium is first-line for CCB overdose, not beta-blockers.
Naloxone reverses opioids, not beta-blockers.

stem: Fomepizole is the antidote for:

choices.feedback: Isopropyl alcohol is metabolized to acetone (not as toxic). Supportive care usually sufficient.

Correct! Fomepizole inhibits alcohol dehydrogenase, preventing metabolism of methanol/ethylene glycol into toxic metabolites (formic acid from methanol → blindness; oxalic acid from ethylene glycol → renal failure). Give early — before toxic metabolites accumulate. Also: hemodialysis for severe cases.
Ethanol intoxication is treated supportively, not with fomepizole.
NAC is for acetaminophen.

stem: Activated charcoal is most effective when given:

choices.feedback: Inducing emesis is no longer recommended.

Correct! Activated charcoal adsorbs most drugs in the GI tract but must be given early (within 1-2h, ideally <1h). Contraindicated: altered mental status without protected airway (aspiration risk), caustic/acid ingestion, hydrocarbons. Does NOT adsorb: metals (iron, lithium, lead), alcohols, acids/alkalis. Single dose for most ingestions.
After 2 hours, most ingested material has left the stomach.
Efficacy drops dramatically after 1-2 hours.

stem: Digoxin toxicity is treated with:

choices.feedback: High-dose insulin is for CCB/BB overdose, not digoxin.
Bicarb is for TCA and salicylate overdose, not digoxin.
Calcium is generally AVOIDED in digoxin toxicity (may worsen cardiac toxicity — 'stone heart' phenomenon, though evidence is debated).

Correct! Digoxin-specific Fab fragments bind free digoxin → rapid reversal. Indications: life-threatening arrhythmias, hyperkalemia (K >5), hemodynamic instability. Also correct: treat hyperkalemia (but avoid calcium), atropine for bradycardia, do NOT cardiovert (may precipitate VF). Classic findings: 'scooped' ST segments, any arrhythmia (especially bidirectional VT, accelerated junctional rhythm with AV block).
